title: The Shifting Input Problem
concept: As earlier layers keep updating, the hidden activations seen by deeper layers keep drifting, so later layers are learning from moving targets instead of steady inputs.

exercises.prompt: Recap: In a deep network, what does each hidden layer receive from the layer before it?

options.text: A set of activations that becomes the next layer's input.
A brand-new dataset collected during training.
A finished final prediction with no more processing needed.

exercises.explain: Correct. Each hidden layer depends on the activations produced by the layer before it.

cards.title: Practicing on a moving target
cards.body: Imagine learning archery while the bullseye slides a little after every shot. Even if you improve, the target keeps shifting under you. Deeper neural network layers face a similar problem when earlier layers keep changing the signals they send forward.

cards.title: Why hidden inputs keep drifting
cards.body: During training, earlier layers are constantly updating their weights. That means the hidden activations they produce can change from one step to the next. Later layers must keep adapting to these moving inputs. This instability is often called internal covariate shift.

cards.title: A teacher who keeps changing the rubric
cards.body: Suppose you are writing essays, but the teacher changes the grading rubric every week. One week structure matters most, the next week word count, then tone. You are not just learning the task — you are relearning the rules. Later layers feel that same frustration when earlier layers keep changing their output style.

cards.title: Layer 3 keeps readjusting
cards.body: Say Layer 2 first sends activations mostly between 0 and 2, so Layer 3 starts learning useful weights for that range. After a few updates, Layer 2 now sends values mostly between -3 and 5. Layer 3 has to readjust again, even if the overall task did not change.

cards.title: The dataset is not what's shifting
cards.body: This does not mean the raw training data suddenly changed. The images, words, or numbers in the dataset are the same. What keeps shifting is the inside of the network: the hidden-layer outputs seen by later layers.

cards.title: Stable inputs vs. drifting inputs
cards.body: Why deeper layers have an easier or harder job:

left.text:
Next layer sees a familiar range.
Weights can refine steadily.
Learning feels more predictable.

right.text:
Next layer keeps seeing new scales.
Weights must relearn what values mean.
Training becomes harder to stabilize.

exercises.prompt: What is the main problem in internal covariate shift?

options.text: Later layers keep receiving hidden inputs whose distribution changes as earlier layers learn.
The dataset deletes old examples after each batch.
The output layer stops using gradients entirely.
Every hidden layer becomes an input layer.

exercises.explain: Exactly. The issue is that deeper layers keep seeing their inputs drift while previous layers update.

exercises.prompt: True or false: If earlier layers change a lot during training, deeper layers may need to keep re-learning how to interpret their inputs.
exercises.explain: True. When hidden activations shift, later layers have to keep adjusting to the new patterns and scales.

exercises.sentence: For deeper layers, internal covariate shift can feel like learning on a moving ___.
exercises.explain: Right. The common intuition is a moving target: the task is similar, but the incoming signals keep drifting.

exercises.prompt: Which scenario best matches the shifting input problem?

options.text: A deeper layer learns useful weights, then earlier layers update and suddenly send activations on a very different scale, forcing that deeper layer to readjust.
A training set is saved to disk after the epoch ends.
A network makes one prediction and then retires forever.

exercises.explain: Yes. The hallmark is that later layers must keep reinterpreting changed hidden activations from earlier layers.

exercises.prompt: A speech model's fourth layer had started learning useful patterns when the third layer's updates suddenly changed its output range and average activation. What is the biggest training consequence for the fourth layer?

options.text: It must keep re-adjusting because the meaning and scale of its inputs have shifted under it.
It instantly becomes the output layer and stops needing weights.
It no longer receives any activations from earlier layers.
It automatically memorizes the new range with no learning cost.

exercises.explain: Exactly. This is the core pain point: deeper layers are not learning on steady ground. Next, we'll see the tool designed to calm those hidden activations down: batch normalization.
